The apostle directly addresses those who promoted the law of Moses alongside the gospel of Christ, attempting to bring believers under its control. They did not fully understand the meaning of the law as given by Moses. Since that was a time of darkness and bondage, they were tied to many burdensome rituals and practices, by which they were taught and kept subject like a child under tutors and guardians. We learn about the happier state of Christians under the gospel dispensation. From these verses, see the wonders of Divine love and mercy; particularly of God the Father, in sending his Son into the world to redeem and save us; of the Son of God, in submitting so low and suffering so much for us; and of the Holy Spirit, in condescending to dwell in the hearts of believers, for such gracious purposes. Also, see the advantages Christians enjoy under the gospel. Although by nature children of wrath and disobedience, they become by grace children of love, and share in the nature of God’s children; for he will have all his children resemble him. Among people, the oldest son is heir; but all God’s children will have the inheritance of eldest sons. May our character and behavior always show our adoption; and may the Holy Spirit confirm within us that we are children and heirs of God.

The change whereby the Galatians were turned from idols to the living God, and through Christ had received the adoption of sons, was the result of his free and abundant grace; they were under a greater obligation to remain faithful to the liberty wherewith he had made them free. All our knowledge of God begins with him; we know him because he knows us. Though our religion forbids idolatry, yet many practice spiritual idolatry in their hearts. For whatever a person loves most and cares for most is that person’s god: some have riches as their god, some pleasures, and some lusts. And many ignorantly worship a god of their own making; a god made all of mercy and no justice. For they persuade themselves that there is mercy with God, even though they do not repent but continue in their sins. It is possible for those who have made great professions of religion to be later led astray from purity and simplicity. And the more mercy God has shown in bringing anyone to know the gospel and its liberties and privileges, the greater their sin and folly in allowing themselves to be deprived of them. Therefore, all who are members of the outward church should learn to fear and suspect themselves. We must not be content with good things within ourselves. Paul fears that his labor is in vain, yet he still labors; and thus to do, whatever follows, is true wisdom and the fear of God. This every man must remember in his place and calling.

The apostle desires that they would agree with him regarding the law of Moses, as well as be united with him in love. When correcting others, we should make sure to convince them that our corrections come from sincere regard for the honor of God and religion and their welfare. The apostle reminds the Galatians of the difficulty he faced when he first came among them. He notes that he was a welcome messenger to them. Yet how very uncertain are the favor and respect of people! Let us labor to be accepted by God. You once thought yourselves happy in receiving the gospel; do you now think otherwise? Christians must not refrain from speaking the truth, for fear of offending others. The false teachers who led the Galatians away from the truth of the gospel were scheming men. They pretended affection, but they were not sincere and upright. An excellent rule is given: It is good to be zealous always in a good thing; not just for a time or occasionally, but always. Happy would it be for the church of Christ if this zeal was better maintained.

The Galatians were ready to consider the apostle their enemy, but he assures them that he was their friend; he had parental feelings toward them. He was uncertain about their state and anxious to know the result of their present delusions. Nothing is a more certain proof that a sinner has passed into a state of justification than Christ being formed in him by the renewal of the Holy Spirit; but this cannot be hoped for while people depend on the law for acceptance with God.

The difference between believers who rested in Christ only, and those who trusted in the law, is explained by the histories of Isaac and Ishmael. These things are an allegory, wherein, besides the literal and historical sense of the words, the Spirit of God points out something further. Hagar and Sarah were fitting symbols of the two different covenants. The heavenly Jerusalem, the true church from above, represented by Sarah, is in a state of freedom, and is the mother of all believers, who are born of the Holy Spirit. They were made part of the true seed of Abraham through regeneration and true faith, according to the promise made to him.

The history thus explained is applied. So then, brothers, we are not children of the bondwoman, but of the free. If the privileges of all believers were so great under the new covenant, how absurd for Gentile converts to be under that law, which could not deliver unbelieving Jews from bondage or condemnation! We would not have discovered this allegory in the history of Sarah and Hagar if it had not been shown to us, yet we cannot doubt that it was intended by the Holy Spirit. It is an explanation of the subject, not an argument proving it. The two covenants of works and grace, and legal and evangelical professors, are represented here. Works and fruits brought forth in a person’s own strength are legal. But if arising from faith in Christ, they are evangelical. The first covenant spirit is one of bondage to sin and death. The second covenant spirit is one of liberty and freedom; not liberty to sin, but in and unto duty. The first is a spirit of persecution; the second is a spirit of love. Let those professors consider this who have a violent, harsh, imposing spirit toward the people of God. Yet as Abraham turned aside to Hagar, so it is possible for a believer to turn aside in some things to the covenant of works, when through unbelief and neglect of the promise he acts according to the law, in his own strength; or in a way of violence, not of love, toward the brethren. Yet that is not his way, not his spirit; hence he is never at rest until he returns to dependence on Christ again. Let us rest our souls on the Scriptures, and by a gospel hope and cheerful obedience, show that our conversation and treasure are indeed in heaven.
